practices: The AP Physics C: Mechanics exam assesses three Science Practices. Practice 1: Creating Representations is assessed in the free-response section (20-35% of FRQ score) and involves creating diagrams, graphs, and models to represent physical situations. Practice 2: Mathematical Routines is assessed in both sections (40-45% of FRQ score, approximately 70% of MCQ score) and focuses on deriving expressions, calculating quantities, and predicting changes using mathematical relationships. Practice 3: Scientific Questioning and Argumentation is also assessed in both sections (30-35% of FRQ score, approximately 25% of MCQ score) and requires students to design experiments, analyze data, and justify claims with evidence and physical principles.

keyTopics: Scalars and vectors, including unit vector notation and vector addition
Displacement, instantaneous velocity, and instantaneous acceleration as derivatives and integrals of position and velocity with respect to time
Representing motion using graphs of position, velocity, and acceleration versus time
Kinematic equations for motion with constant acceleration
Reference frames and relative velocity
Motion in two dimensions, including projectile motion
Newton's First, Second, and Third Laws of Motion
Creating and using free-body diagrams to analyze forces
Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation and gravitational fields
Kinetic and static friction
Spring forces and Hooke's Law
Resistive forces as a function of velocity, leading to differential equations and terminal velocity
Center of mass for systems of particles and continuous bodies (using integration)
Uniform circular motion and centripetal acceleration
Work-Energy Theorem
Work done by a constant force (dot product) and by a variable force (integration)
Translational kinetic energy
Conservative vs. nonconservative forces
Potential energy and its relationship to conservative forces (F = -dU/dx)
Conservation of mechanical energy
Gravitational potential energy (near-Earth and universal) and elastic potential energy
Power as the rate of energy transfer (P = dW/dt) and as the dot product of force and velocity (P = F • v)
Linear momentum as a vector quantity
Newton's second law in terms of momentum (F_net = dp/dt)
Impulse and the Impulse-Momentum Theorem (J = ∫F dt = Δp)
Conservation of linear momentum in closed systems
Center-of-mass velocity
Elastic and inelastic collisions in one and two dimensions
Rotational kinematics: angular displacement, velocity, and acceleration
Relationship between linear and angular motion variables
Rotational inertia (moment of inertia) for discrete particles and continuous bodies via integration
Parallel axis theorem
Torque as a vector cross product (τ = r × F)
Newton's second law in rotational form (Στ = Iα)
Rotational equilibrium (Στ = 0)

units.unitTitle: Energy and Momentum of Rotating Systems
keyTopics: Rotational kinetic energy (K = ½Iω²)
Work in rotational motion (W = ∫τ dθ)
Angular momentum for a point particle (L = r × p) and a rigid body (L = Iω)
Conservation of angular momentum
Angular impulse and its relation to the change in angular momentum
Rolling without slipping as a combination of translational and rotational motion
Energy and angular momentum conservation in the motion of orbiting satellites
Defining Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM) through the restoring force (F = -kx)
Differential equation for SHM (d²x/dt² = -ω²x) and its sinusoidal solutions
Period and frequency of SHM
Period of simple pendulums and mass-spring systems
Period of a physical pendulum
Energy conservation in SHM (kinetic and potential energy)

examFormat.freeResponse: 4 questions (Mathematical Routines, Translation Between Representations, Experimental Design and Analysis, Qualitative/Quantitative Translation), 100 minutes, 50% of total score

antiPatterns: The CED explicitly states that students are not expected to know Kepler's first or second laws of planetary motion. Calculus derivations of rotational inertia are limited to specific shapes like thin rods and cylindrical shells/disks. Students are not expected to mathematically prove Newton's shell theorem or that the sinusoidal function is a solution to the SHM differential equation. On free-body diagrams, students should only draw forces, not their components. The analysis of rolling friction, three-dimensional collisions (quantitatively), and rotation in multiple planes simultaneously is out of scope. Unless otherwise stated, reference frames are inertial, air resistance is negligible, and springs/strings are ideal.
